verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What good does it do me that he declares on his oath that it was through simplicity that he went wrong?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: His praises are, as you know, cast in my teeth, and the laudation of this most simple friend (which however has not much either of simplicity or of sincerity in it) is imputed to me as a crime.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If he was seeking a foundation of authority for what he was doing, and wishing to shew who had gone before him in this path he had at hand the Confessor Hilary, who translated the books of Origen upon Job and the Psalms consisting of forty thousand lines.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He had Ambrose whose works are, almost all of them, full of what Origen has written; and the martyr Victorinus, who acts really with ‘simplicity,’ and without setting snares for others.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to all these he keeps silence; he does not notice those who are like pillars of the church; but me, who am but like a flea and a man of no account, he hunts out from corner to corner.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Perhaps the same simplicity which made him unconscious that he was attacking his friend will make him swear that he knew nothing of these writers.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But who will believe that he does not know these men whose memory is quite recent, even though they were Latins, being as he is such a very learned man, and one who has so great a knowledge of the old writers, especially the Greeks, that, in his zeal for foreign knowledge he has almost lost his own language?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The truth is it is not so much that I have been praised by him as that those writers have not been attacked.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But whether what he has written is praise (as he tries to make simpletons believe) or an attack, (as I feel it to be from the pain which his wounds give me), he has taken care that I should have none of my contemporaries to bring me honor by a partnership in praise, nor consolation by a partnership in vituperation.
